sections.title: ## 1. Requirements (Functional + Non-Functional)
sections.summary: Open with the 🎬 structure pitch as a 🎙️ line + one line framing the core tension; list 3-5 core Functional flows 'above the line' (each with: the feature one-liner, '🗣️ Plain words', '⚡ Why it's hard') plus a 'Below the line (out of scope)' list; Non-Functional qualities each with talkable target (9s/p99), '🗣️ Plain words', '💥 What breaks without it'; optional '⚠️ Gaps I'd flag out loud'; close with a 🎙️ Script. TEXT ONLY, no tools.

sections.title: ## 2. Clarifying Questions & Assumptions
sections.summary: For each question, FOUR things: the question as a spoken sentence, '🗣️ Why I'm asking (plain words)', '↔️ Design fork' (If YES.../If NO...), and 'Assumption I'll proceed with'. TEXT ONLY (no tools yet); end with a 🤝 Checkpoint.

sections.title: ## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers)
sections.summary: First tool use allowed. Each number shown as a full visible arithmetic derivation chain in a table: Metric | Derivation (step-by-step math) | Rounded | Talkable phrase | Decision it drives. Gloss every term inline; name the ONE decision-forcing number + its flip threshold; treat storage/non-decision numbers as a half-sentence aside. End with a SHORT 🎙️ Script (2-4 sentences) speaking aloud only the 1-2 decision-driving rounded numbers + the constraint they force.

sections.title: ## 4. Core Entities
sections.summary: For each entity THREE things: 'Entity name' (what it is / what it owns), '🗣️ Simple analogy' (real-world comparison), '⚠️ The interviewer probe' (likely hard question + one-line answer). State this is a first draft with full fields in the data model. Close with a 🎙️ Script naming entities, flagging the most important one, and what the data model will add.

sections.title: ## 5. API / Interface
sections.summary: Go one-by-one through functional requirements (usually 1:1 endpoints). For each: Method + path (why this HTTP verb), 2-4 key request fields (fieldName: type + why; call out intentionally absent server-set fields), a concrete JSON request+response example with realistic values, and one key design decision (idempotency key, PENDING vs sync, redirect vs JSON). Default REST; security note (identify caller from session/JWT, never trust client ids/prices). 💬 note + 🎙️ Script walking the endpoints.

sections.title: ## 6. High-Level Design (built one functional requirement at a time)
sections.summary: One ### 6.x subsection per above-the-line requirement, in priority order, starting minimal. Each: one-sentence framing; ARCHITECTURE DECISIONS TABLE (Component | What it is plain English | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off accepted, 2-5 rows) + a 🎙️ narration; a '🗣️ Key terms for this slice' GLOSSES BLOCK (one standalone bolded entry per term); a focused mermaid flowchart TD diagram (skip with a '↳ builds on §6.x' note if no new components); NUMBERED STEP NARRATION (action / why this component / state change / what could go wrong); a 💬 say-while-drawing line; a 🎙️ Script. Ends with the ONE 'Final (high-level)' diagram that is the exact reconciled union of all slices + 🎙️ Script + 🤝 Checkpoint.

sections.title: ## 7. Data Model & Storage
sections.summary: PART A entity table (Entity | Key fields with PK/shard key annotated | Chosen store | Shard/partition key | Consistency guarantee); PART B a standalone storage decision card per distinct store (What it is plain words / Why we picked it - the access pattern that forces it / What we considered & rejected with concrete reasons / Trade-off accepted / 🗣️ How to say it out loud); PART C per-operation consistency summary table (Operation | Store | Consistency level | Why right here, covering strong writes, eventual reads, read-your-own-writes). 🎙️ Script of 4-6 sentences with one 'considered X but rejected because Y'.

sections.title: ## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great (the senior signal)
sections.summary: Where the interview is won. Open with a 🆘 'If you get stuck' pointer. Pick 3-5 hardest problems, each posed as '### How do we [problem]?'. Escalate through Bad/Good/Great tiers, each with fixed structure: (Good/Great) '↩️ What the previous tier got wrong', '🗣️ What this is in plain words', 'Approach' (mechanism, terms defined inline), 'Why people try this', '⚠️ What breaks (Challenges)' with concrete numbers, '🔁 What forces the upgrade'. Good/Great get a tier-specific mechanism diagram; Great adds '🔢 Decision-forcing math' and a '✅ Failure matrix'. Each deep dive ends with a 🎙️ Script + 🧠 'If they ask...'. Tiers optional. Covers bottleneck/relief/new ceiling, hot-key/celebrity, idempotency, global coordinators, consistency + read-your-own-writes, failure behavior.

sections.title: ## 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost
sections.summary: Four named sub-sections with plain-English term definitions: 9A Availability targets (nines per path + human-terms downtime + mechanisms like multi-AZ); 9B per-dependency failure table (Component | What breaks | How it degrades gracefully (what the USER SEES) | Recovery action); 9C RPO/RTO per data class (define RPO/RTO in plain words first, then table Data class | RPO | RTO | Mechanism); 9D cost breakdown (top 3 cost drivers + why each is expensive + rough monthly order-of-magnitude + the one biggest optimization). 🎙️ Script of 3-4 sentences on what the user experiences when things fail.

sections.title: ## 10. Trade-off Ledger
sections.summary: For each of the 2-4 most significant decisions, a DECISION CARD '🔀 Decision: [chose] vs [didn't]' with five parts: What we chose and why / What we gave up / 🗣️ Plain words / When this reverses (exact flip condition) / 🗣️ How to say it. Tie back to non-functional requirements and any §1 '⚠️ Gaps'. 🎙️ Script of 3-4 sentences walking the ledger and what would change your mind.

sections.title: ## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers
sections.summary: Cover all 7 domains (core algorithm/hardest part, failure scenarios, scale/hot-spot, consistency/correctness, security, cost, extensibility), 2-3 per domain, 12-18 total. Each question: '❓ [question verbatim]', 'The mechanism (what actually happens)' 2-3 sentences with inline term definitions, '🗣️ In plain words' jargon-free fallback, '💬 One-liner to say out loud'. Answers must be real and specific with numbers. Ends with a 🎙️ '60-second verbal summary' in flowing connected sentences following the read/write-halves template.

prompt.diagramsExpected:
Diagrams are MANDATORY and central, emitted as fenced ```mermaid blocks (render to SVG). Diagram kinds by purpose: flowchart TD (the DEFAULT for high-level design / layered architecture and multi-path flows — never flowchart LR for multi-path HLD because it stretches off a 13-inch screen; LR only for a dead-simple 3-4 node linear chain), sequenceDiagram (request/interaction flow), classDiagram (class structure, LLD), erDiagram (data models, though a Markdown table is preferred). WHERE: Section 6 is the diagram-heavy section — EVERY §6.x slice subsection gets its OWN focused diagram + numbered narration (skip only with an explicit '↳ builds on §6.x diagram — no new components' note when the slice adds no new components), and §6 ends with exactly ONE 'Final (high-level)' diagram that is the reconciled union of all slices (nothing dropped, nothing new). Section 8 deep dives: each meaningful tier (usually Good and Great) gets a titled mermaid diagram showing ONLY that tier's mechanism, not the whole system. So roughly one diagram per functional requirement (3-5) + one final union diagram + ~2 mechanism diagrams per deep dive across 3-5 deep dives. CONVENTIONS (strict): use the hourglass/diamond layout (single entry node at top with NO subgraph wrapper, two side-by-side subgraphs for parallel paths e.g. '⚡ Read path' vs '🔄 Async/Write path', shared storage nodes below outside subgraphs, cold/fallback at the very bottom); edges between entry node and subgraphs go OUTSIDE the subgraph defs to force side-by-side rendering. SAME ENTITY SAME EVERYWHERE — a component keeps the same node id, label, and color in every diagram. Semantic color palette defined once and reused: client=blue (#4A90D9 / #1a3a5c fill), svc/our services=green (#2E7D4F / #2ea043), async/queues=amber (#B7791F), store/datastores=grey (#4A5568 / #6B7280) drawn as cylinders [(name)], external=purple (#6B46C1 / #a78bfa); all fills dark enough for white text. Cap ~10-12 nodes per diagram (split if bigger). EVERY edge must have a SHORT label including a step number AND 2-4 word description (e.g. -->|\"3: read leaderboard\"|), numbered 1:1 with the prose steps; dashed -.-> for async/fire-and-forget. Use subgraph only when it genuinely clarifies. linkStyle to distinguish path types (green thick for sync/hot read, amber dashed for async, grey dashed for fallback/cache-miss, blue thick for entry). One bold caption line above each diagram, plus a one-line intro and a words explanation after. MERMAID SAFETY: keep labels free of breaking chars ( ) [ ] | : / \" < > and curly braces; use
not raw newlines; in erDiagram write attributes as just 'type name' with no quoted comments or enum value lists.
prompt.capacityEstimation: Capacity/back-of-envelope estimation lives in Section 3 'Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers)' and is the FIRST place tool use is allowed (tools must NOT be called before the opener, Sections 1-2, are fully emitted as text). Arithmetic must be COMPUTED exactly by writing Python to a file (Write, with a relative path like capacity.py) and running it via Bash (python3 capacity.py) — never hand-waved, never shown unrun. Present each number as a table row with the FULL visible derivation chain so the candidate can explain HOW not just WHAT: format 'Metric | Derivation (step-by-step math) | Rounded | Talkable phrase | Decision it drives' — e.g. '1B users × 0.1% DAU = 1M DAU; 1M × 10 reads/day ÷ 86,400s ≈ 116/s → round to ~120/s'. Only compute the few numbers that actually influence the design (skip vanity 'it's a lot' math). EVERY number must be ROUNDED to a memorable, say-out-loud figure (~500K writes/s, ~100:1 read:write, ~$200K/mo, ~16K QPS) with NO false precision (never '15,625 QPS' or '$197,932/mo'). Feature the ONE number that forces a design choice and the exact threshold where that choice flips (e.g. 'a Redis node tops out ~150K ops/s, we're at 500K → shard'). Math written in PLAIN TEXT only (no LaTeX, no \\frac, no $...$): use '~', 'x' for multiply, '÷', '62^7 ≈ 3.5 trillion'. Specific rigor invariants govern it: size the read tier from MISS QPS not cache size (defended hit-ratio → residual DB QPS → replica/primary counts → cold-cache 0% worst case); storage = full footprint (per-row bytes + every index/secondary table + replication factor + WAL/compaction 3-6x headroom + the largest/events table + rough monthly $ naming the dominant driver, often egress/CDN or OLAP not the primary DB); decompose the p99 budget across DNS/TLS/CDN/LB/app/cache/DB hops so it sums to the SLO, separating cache-hit from cache-miss and cross-region paths. Treat storage and non-decision numbers as a half-sentence aside ('storage's a non-issue, a few TB'). End with a SHORT 🎙️ Script (2-4 sentences) speaking aloud ONLY the one or two decision-driving heavily-rounded numbers ('about 8 thousand reads a second') + the ratio/constraint they force + the conclusion ('so I'll spend my budget on reads, not writes').
mustHaves: API design — Section 5: REST by default, method+path with HTTP-verb justification, 2-4 key request fields with why, intentionally-absent server-set fields called out, concrete JSON request+response examples with realistic values, one key design decision per endpoint, security (caller from session/JWT, never trust client ids/prices)
Data model — Section 7: entity table with PK/shard key annotated + chosen store + partition key + consistency guarantee; storage decision card per store; per-operation consistency summary table
Scaling strategy + capacity math — Section 3: computed (Python via Bash), full derivations, rounded talkable numbers, the one decision-driving number + its flip threshold

Bottlenecks — name the ONE binding bottleneck under peak (including hot-key/celebrity single-shard QPS), quantify its ceiling, show the relief and the NEW ceiling (rigor invariant 8; surfaced in §8 deep dives)
Trade-offs — Section 10 Trade-off Ledger: 2-4 decision cards (chose vs didn't / gave up / plain words / when it reverses / how to say it), tied back to non-functional reqs and §1 gaps
Deep dives Bad→Good→Great — Section 8: 3-5 hardest problems, each escalated through tiers with mechanism diagrams, decision-forcing math, and a failure matrix for correctness-critical ones
Idempotency on EVERY mutating endpoint (rigor invariant 7): idempotency/natural key + dedup store with TTL, what a retried request returns, no shared-namespace collisions
Examine EVERY global coordinator (rigor invariant 9): counter/allocator/sequencer/lock/leader/quorum — its throughput ceiling, cold-start/partition behavior, what invariant breaks when down

Consistency per operation and path, not one global label (rigor invariant 6): explicitly trace read-your-own-writes across cache miss, lagging replica, far region, cold edge; state staleness bound + mitigation

Multi-region / async (rigor invariant 10): geo-routing + write topology (single-write-region vs multi-master), cross-region latency + replica-lag, delivery guarantee (at-most/at-least/exactly-once) tied to an accuracy SLA with idempotent consumer

Reliability/DR/security/ops as first-class — Section 9 (rigor invariant 11): availability in 9s, per-dependency graceful degradation, RPO/RTO per data class with tested restore, multi-AZ vs multi-region failover triggers, deploy/rollback + reversible migrations, authn/authz + abuse defense + PII/retention/right-to-erasure, SLO burn-rate alerts + tracing + runbooks
Cost — Section 9D and rigor: top 3 cost drivers with plain why, rough monthly order-of-magnitude, the one biggest optimization
SLOs/objective as numbers up front (rigor invariant 4): target percentile, scope/region, window, and what you are NOT optimizing; revisit at the end
🎙️ SAY-IT Scripts at the end of (nearly) every section — connected first-person read-aloud narration (~3-6 sentences) that alone form a complete spoken walkthrough
🎬 Structure pitch in the opener (the 🎙️ Script line where candidate states the ~40-minute plan)
🧠 Jargon guard — every tech/algorithm/acronym glossed in plain English the first time it appears, plus '🧠 If they ask' defense lines for load-bearing choices
🤝 Checkpoints at exactly the three seams (after Requirements, after high-level design, before deep dives)
🆘 Recovery lines block right before the deep dives (don't-know-a-tech / are-you-sure / you-blank)
⚠️ Traps — problem-specific candidate-sinking mistakes (wrong move → why it sinks you → right move), woven where each bites

🏢 Company x Level lens — state the company/level assumption in the opener and tilt the leading deep dive, the featured number, and vocabulary (here: Amazon SDE-3 — cost & operations first-class, work backwards from customer, decide and move; LPs Bias for Action and Learn and Be Curious)
Section 11 Likely Interviewer Questions — all 7 domains, 12-18 Q&A, ending with the 🎙️ 60-second verbal summary
prompt.toneAndLength: TONE: First-person and collaborative whiteboard 'teachable senior peer' voice (Hello-Interview style) — 'Let's...', 'We'll...', 'Now we need to...', inviting the interviewer in. Incremental and anti-complexity: start small, explicitly resist early scaling ('Let me resist adding a cache here — we'll earn it in the deep dives'). Decisive but not dogmatic: state a choice plus its trade-off. Plain-English first, then the precise term. Numbers always rounded and say-out-loud-able. Heavily layered for SPEAKING ALOUD: every major section ends with a connected first-person 🎙️ Script (~3-6 sentences, contractions, no editing needed); short 💬 blockquote 'say THIS while you draw' lines throughout; 🗣️ plain-words / layman explanations for any jargon-heavy concept; emojis are part of the prescribed structure (🎬 🎙️ 💬 🗣️ 🧠 🤝 🆘 ⚠️ ⚡ 💥 🔀 ❓ 🗄️ ↔️ ↩️ ✅ 🔢 🔁). LATENCY CONSTRAINT: it runs in a LIVE interview, so the speakable opener (Sections 1-2: problem frame + 🎬 structure pitch, functional reqs, non-functional reqs, clarifying questions + assumptions) must stream FIRST as plain text with NO extended thinking and NO tool calls before it; deeper reasoning and tools are deferred to the Section 3 capacity math onward. LENGTH: long, comprehensive, single-pass — 11 full sections covering everything a senior interviewer might probe, but scripts kept tight (a spine to speak from, not a transcript). MULTI-TURN: after the first complete answer, follow-ups are answered directly and concisely; if asked to change/extend, re-output the full updated solution. CODE/PSEUDOCODE: this is HLD, so there is NO application/algorithm pseudocode in the answer body — code appears only in two narrow places: (1) realistic JSON request/response example blocks in the API section (Section 5), and (2) Python that the model itself writes to a file and RUNS via Bash to compute capacity numbers exactly (Section 3) — that Python is a verification/computation tool, with only its rounded results surfaced, never unrun code shown. Output is pure Markdown (tables with real line breaks, fenced code blocks with language tags, plain-text math, no LaTeX, no stray XML/tool tags).

recipeSteps: Step 0 (prerequisite): Read the full HLD system prompt from system_design_prompt.md (recipe says /Users/anshullkgarg/Desktop/projects/claude-gpt/system_design_prompt.md; real path is /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/system_design_prompt.md) -- it is the exact production prompt and the output must match every section it describes, ss1 through ss11.

Step 1 -- Pick a session ID: use a fresh UUID4. Session ID format is '<uuid>:hld'. Example: 'a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890:hld'. Compute the slug = re.sub(r'[^A-Za-z0-9_-]', '_', session_id), which turns ':hld' into '_hld' (used for the file path).

Step 2 -- Generate the full ss1-ss11 HLD response as markdown using the system prompt. This is what the user reads and must be fully expanded with no shortcuts. All 11 required sections: ss1 Requirements (layman parenthetical + 'why it's hard' per requirement, 'what breaks without it' per NFR); ss2 Clarifying Questions (6-10 per category, default assumption + design fork If YES/If NO, plain-words speaker line); ss3 Capacity Estimation (full derivation chains, every acronym glossed e.g. QPS = Queries Per Second, final summary table metric/value/raw calc); ss4 Data Model (entity role, field table with types, spoken intro, analogy, interviewer probe per entity); ss5 API Design (endpoint table method/path/auth, request+response fields with WHY, narration per endpoint); ss6 High-Level Architecture (mermaid diagram in ```mermaid fence, 5-column decision table component/options/chosen/why/plain-words/rejected, subgraph per flow, 4-part step narration for >=2 flows); ss7 Data Model & Storage (3-part decision card: what is this / what we rejected / how to say it; cover primary DB, cache, blob/object storage, search index if relevant); ss8 Deep Dives (6-part tier structure: what prev tier got wrong / how this fixes it / decision math / failure modes / failure matrix failure->impact->mitigation / narration; cover caching, sharding/replication, async processing, CDN/global); ss9 Reliability & Fault Tolerance (define RPO and RTO in plain English before stating targets; 4 subsections: SPOFs+elimination, sync vs async replication, circuit breakers/retries/backoff, disaster recovery); ss10 Trade-off Ledger (5-part card: choice / what gave up / why / when this reverses / how to say it; cover consistency vs availability, SQL vs NoSQL, sync vs async, monolith vs microservices); ss11 Likely Interviewer Questions (all 7 domains: algorithm/DS, failure handling, scale/hot-spot, consistency, security, cost, extensibility; 4-part answer per question: one-line answer / supporting detail / trade-off ack / interview phrasing; close with 60-second verbal summary template).

Step 3 -- Write the conversation JSON to data/conversations/<slug>.json (recipe path /Users/anshullkgarg/Desktop/projects/claude-gpt/data/conversations/<slug>.json; real path /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/data/conversations/<slug>.json). Filename uses <uuid>_hld.json (underscore); session_id inside JSON uses <uuid>:hld (colon). Recipe gives a Python snippet using json/pathlib/time/uuid that writes json.dumps(data, ensure_ascii=False, indent=2) encoded utf-8.

Step 4 -- Verify with the provided Python assertion script: assert file exists, d['session_id'] == sid, d['mode'] == 'hld', d['transcript'][1]['blocks'][0]['k'] == 'text', and len(d['transcript'][1]['blocks'][0]['md']) > 10000. Prints 'OK' and the open URL. Final open URL: https://localhost:8002/?s=<session_id> (i.e. <uuid>:hld).

Final -- Run the full quality checklist (16 items) confirming all 11 sections present/expanded and each section's required sub-structure, the JSON exists, session_id uses :hld, the slug filename uses _hld, and the markdown text block is > 10,000 characters.
builder.fileOrWorkspaceNeeds: HLD generation is essentially markdown-only for the design content; it writes exactly ONE workspace file: the conversation JSON at data/conversations/<slug>.json. The recipe explicitly states 'HLD sessions do NOT have Python workspace files -- they are entirely prose, tables, and diagrams.' No code is executed/run as part of the deliverable -- the only commands are (a) the Python snippet in Step 3 that writes the JSON file, and (b) the Step 4 Python verification script that reads/asserts the JSON. There is no app build, no test run, no Python workspace/solution files. The system_design_prompt.md is only read, not written.
builder.jsonContract: session_id form: '<uuid>:hld' (colon) inside the JSON; the on-disk filename uses the slug '<uuid>_hld.json' (colon replaced by underscore via re.sub(r'[^A-Za-z0-9_-]', '_', session_id)). Top-level JSON object fields (verbatim): 'session_id' (= '<uuid>:hld'), 'mode' (= 'hld'), 'title' (= 'Design <System Name>'), 'created_at' (unix timestamp int), 'updated_at' (created_at + 120), 'messages' (= [] -- empty array), 'transcript' (array). NOTE: there is BOTH a 'messages' key (always empty []) AND a 'transcript' key; the content lives in 'transcript'. transcript[] holds exactly two entries: [0] the user turn = {'role':'user','text':'Design <System Name>','images':0}; [1] the assistant turn = {'role':'assistant','thinking':'' (empty string), 'blocks':[ {'k':'text','md':'<THE FULL ss1-ss11 MARKDOWN RESPONSE>'} ], 'usage':{'input_tokens':8192,'output_tokens':18000,'cache_read_input_tokens':24000,'cache_creation_input_tokens':0}}. blocks[] uses a single block with key 'k' = 'text' and key 'md' holding the entire markdown response. NO tool blocks (no write_file, no code/exec blocks) are used anywhere in hld mode -- only the single {'k':'text','md':...} block. Written via json.dumps(data, ensure_ascii=False, indent=2).
gotchas: PATH MISMATCH: the recipe hardcodes old paths under /Users/anshullkgarg/Desktop/projects/claude-gpt/ (for system_design_prompt.md, data/conversations/, and the cd in Step 4). The real project root is /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora. Both /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/system_design_prompt.md and /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/data/conversations/ exist and are confirmed -- use the gbang paths.

COLON vs UNDERSCORE is the central gotcha and is called out twice: session_id INSIDE the JSON must use ':hld' (colon); the FILENAME slug must use '_hld' (colon -> underscore). Mixing these breaks session opening.
The slug is computed with re.sub(r'[^A-Za-z0-9_-]', '_', session_id) -- every non-alphanumeric char except '-' and '_' becomes '_'.
The markdown 'md' block MUST be > 10,000 characters; the Step 4 verifier asserts len(...) > 10000 and the checklist restates 'must be thorough'. Abbreviated/short responses fail.

ALL 11 sections must be present and FULLY EXPANDED (not abbreviated) -- the checklist enforces per-section structure (e.g. ss6 needs a ```mermaid``` diagram + 5-column decision table + 4-part narration for >=2 flows; ss9 must define RPO/RTO in plain English BEFORE giving target values; ss11 must cover all 7 question domains and end with a 60-second verbal summary template).
The JSON must contain BOTH an empty 'messages': [] array AND the populated 'transcript' array -- don't drop 'messages'.

Emoji markers are part of the spec the response must include (speaker emoji for spoken/narration lines, mic emoji for spoken intros in ss4). Write JSON with ensure_ascii=False so they survive, indent=2.

Must read system_design_prompt.md FIRST -- it is described as 'the exact prompt the AI uses in production' and the output must match every section (ss1-ss11) it describes. Do not generate from memory.
Final open URL uses the colon form: https://localhost:8002/?s=<uuid>:hld (port 8002, https, localhost).
assistant turn fields 'thinking' is an empty string '' (not null/omitted), and 'usage' carries fixed placeholder token counts (input 8192, output 18000, cache_read 24000, cache_creation 0).

frontend.hldModeRendering: hld is NOT a special render path — the frontend has no `if (mode === "hld")` branch anywhere. `mode` is just a per-tab/session string (frontend/app.js line 78 `hld: makeTab("hld")`, payload field `mode: tab.mode` at line 552, and PLACEHOLDERS.hld line 56). hld assistant turns are rendered by the SAME generic functions every mode uses: live via `handleEvent`/`finalizeText`/`scheduleRender` and on restore via `renderSavedAssistant` (app.js 976-1003). The ONLY place mode is read in the renderer is `attachFullCode(turn, mode)` (app.js 718): `if (mode === "lld")` renders the inline tabbed code viewer; for every OTHER mode (hld, assessment, interview, behavioral) it falls to `if (btn) btn.hidden = !hasFiles;` — i.e. hld gets the "📂 full code" MODAL button (shown only when the turn captured write_file files), never the inline viewer. So "detecting hld" effectively = "the saved JSON has mode:'hld' and is restored into the hld tab"; rendering is identical to assessment/interview/behavioral. Quick-action follow-up buttons are the other mode-keyed bit: QUICK_ACTIONS.hld (app.js 321-326: "Scale 10x", "Security", "DB Deep Dive", "Failure Modes") are appended after the turn by showQuickActions(tab) keyed on tab.mode.
frontend.conversationJsonShape: Source of truth = backend/store.py save() + TranscriptBuilder. A saved hld conversation file (data/conversations/<uuid>_hld.json) has TOP-LEVEL KEYS exactly: {session_id, mode, title, created_at, updated_at, messages, transcript} (store.py save lines 39-47). mode must be "hld". `messages[]` = raw Anthropic agent messages used to CONTINUE the conversation (not used for display). `transcript[]` = the render-ready array the UI replays; it alternates items by `role`. USER item: {role:"user", text:<string>, images:<int count>} (TranscriptBuilder.add_user line 113; frontend reads it.role/it.text/it.images at app.js 1023-1024). ASSISTANT item: {role:"assistant", thinking:<string>, blocks:[...], usage:<obj|null>} (TranscriptBuilder.items 154-159). blocks[] uses a discriminant field "k" with three values: (1) {k:"text", md:<markdown string>} (store.py _flush_text line 117); (2) {k:"tool", id:<string>, name:<tool name>, input:<obj>, output:<string|null>, is_error:<bool>} (store.py 133-140); (3) {k:"web", query:<string>} (store.py 130). The frontend dispatches on b.k in renderSavedAssistant (app.js 987 `b.k === "text"` -> renderMarkdown(b.md), 992 `b.k === "tool"` -> addToolCall+addToolResult using b.id/b.name/b.input/b.output/b.is_error, 996 `b.k === "web"` -> addWebSearch(b.query)). item.thinking renders into a collapsed think-block; item.usage renders a usage line. Confirmed against 060c4a0e..._hld.json: top keys match exactly, mode:"hld", one assistant turn with 5 blocks (kinds: 3 tool, 2 text).
frontend.toolBlocksUsed: hld DOES use write_file/run_command/run_python tool blocks — it is NOT a single markdown block. Survey of all 44 saved *_hld.json files: 24 of 44 contain at least one {k:"tool"} block; across all hld files there are 79 text blocks and 55 tool blocks. The tools are typically run_python/write_file used for back-of-envelope capacity/scale estimation (e.g. 060c4a0e_hld.json has run_python (is_error:true), write_file, run_python). A fresh hld answer is therefore a MIX: mostly {k:"text"} markdown blocks (with embedded mermaid fences) interleaved with optional {k:"tool"} blocks. Tool blocks render exactly as in other modes via addToolCall/addToolResult (TOOL_META icons, "running/done/error" badge, collapsible body; write_file content also captured into turn.files which, in hld, powers the "📂 full code" MODAL — not the lld inline viewer). If you hand-author a minimal valid hld JSON you can use a single {k:"text"} block (the renderer handles 0 tool blocks fine — attachFullCode just hides the full-code button), but a realistic hld transcript contains multiple text blocks plus 1-3 tool blocks for capacity math.
frontend.diagramRendering:
Mermaid is mode-agnostic and triggered purely by a ```mermaid fenced code block inside a text block's `md` string (confirmed: all 44 hld files contain a mermaid fence in their text md). Pipeline: renderMarkdown (markdown.js 196) extracts fenced blocks and calls renderCodeBlock(lang, code) (markdown.js 57); when language==="mermaid" (line 62) it emits NOT a code-block but `
...source...
` (line 64). The SVG conversion happens in renderMermaidIn(root) (markdown.js 509): it finds `.mermaid-block:not([data-done])`, lazy-loads the vendored lib via ensureMermaid() (/vendor/mermaid.min.js), base64-decodes data-code, calls `mermaid.render(el.dataset.id, code)` and injects the svg (line 522-523); on parse failure it retries once with breaking chars stripped from quoted labels, else adds class "mermaid-error" leaving the 
 source visible as fallback. renderMermaidIn is invoked for hld in BOTH live (handleEvent turn_done -> `renderMermaidIn(tab.turn.body)`, app.js 507; also finalizeText line 287) and restore (renderSavedAssistant -> `renderMermaidIn(turn.body)`, app.js 1000). Rendered blocks get class "rendered" (cursor:zoom-in + "⤢ tap to enlarge" hint, styles.css 771-780) and a fullscreen tap-to-zoom overlay (.mermaid-zoom). No hld-specific mermaid handling — identical to lld/interview.
frontend.differencesFromLld: The ONLY rendering difference between hld and lld lives in attachFullCode(turn, mode) (app.js 718-729): for mode==="lld" it hides the "📂 full code" button and calls renderInlineFullCode(turn), which builds the inline ".inline-fullcode" / "Complete Code Implementation" section — a hellointerview-style TABBED code viewer (.ifc-viewer/.ifc-tabs/.ifc-pane, app.js 735-801, styled styles.css 871-965) sourced from turn.files (the write_file captures). hld does NOT get this inline tabbed viewer; instead it falls to the generic else branch `if (btn) btn.hidden = !hasFiles;` so hld shows the "📂 full code" MODAL button (openCodeModal, app.js 809) only when the turn wrote files — the same modal assessment/interview/behavioral use. Everything else is shared and identical: markdown prose (.prose) with the rich blockquote semantics (🎙️ script-block, talk/check/sos/ask/trap/voice/hard/warn/fork/prev/math/ok/upgrade/tradeoff classes, styles.css 639-707), tier cards (Bad/Good/Great, styles.css 709-732), tables, mermaid diagrams with tap-to-zoom, tool cards, reasoning block, usage line. The other mode-keyed difference is non-render: QUICK_ACTIONS differ per mode (hld = Scale 10x / Security / DB Deep Dive / Failure Modes vs lld = Design Patterns / Sequence Diagram / Error Handling / Unit Tests). Net: an hld answer is essentially "rich rendered markdown (with mermaid) + optional tool cards + a full-code MODAL button," whereas lld additionally embeds the inline tabbed code viewer.

existing.freshHldExample: session_id: 99ed5849-fdc2-45de-bf0c-94ade313204a:hld | filename: /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/data/conversations/99ed5849-fdc2-45de-bf0c-94ade313204a_hld.json (title: "Design a Movie Ticket Booking Service (BookMyShow / TicketMaster)", updated_at 1781903616 — the single most recent file). ACTUAL STRUCTURE: Top-level keys (7): session_id, mode, title, created_at, updated_at, messages, transcript. mode="hld". session_id ends in ":hld". messages is an EMPTY list []. The content lives entirely in transcript, which is a list of exactly 2 entries. transcript[0] = user turn with keys {role:"user", text:<the prompt string>, images:0 (an int count, not a list)}. transcript[1] = assistant turn with keys {role:"assistant", thinking:"" (empty string), blocks:[...], usage:{input_tokens, output_tokens, cache_read_input_tokens, cache_creation_input_tokens}}. transcript[1].blocks in this fresh file = exactly ONE block, shaped {"k":"text","md":"<entire HLD markdown>"}. NOTE the block schema uses keys k+md (NOT type/text). The single md string here is ~54.5K chars and holds the ENTIRE answer: 10 H2 sections ("## 1. Requirements"... "## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions") plus H3 subsections. Mermaid is embedded INLINE inside that one md string as ```mermaid fenced code blocks (5 of them here, all `flowchart TD`), each placed right after a bold label line like **Browse flow** / **Final (high-level)**. There are NO tool blocks in fresh files. (Tool blocks shaped {"k":"tool","id","name","input","output","is_error"} appear ONLY in the older generation of files, ranks 20-43 by recency.)
